title: Listening - Part 4: Creating Sounds for Films
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Ben, who creates sound effects for films. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest this morning is Ben Carter, a Foley artist, which means he creates sound effects for films. Ben, how did you discover this unusual work? || Ben: At college I was studying film editing, not sound. A friend had made a short animated film and discovered it seemed strangely empty without everyday noises. [24]I offered to create sounds for the animation using objects in my kitchen, and I enjoyed that part far more than editing pictures[/24]. Until then I hadn’t realised it could be a career. || Presenter: Don’t films simply use sounds stored on computers? || Ben: Recorded libraries are useful for certain things, like distant traffic. But a film feels more believable when effects are made specially for its action. [25]Most of my work involves recording real objects in time with the pictures rather than selecting ready-made sounds from a computer[/25]. We might do twenty versions before one feels right. || Presenter: Footsteps must be simple, surely? || Ben: They are the most common sound and among the hardest. You need the correct shoes and surface, of course, but that isn’t enough. [26]Footsteps have to show whether a character is confident, tired or trying not to be heard[/26]. Two people walking across the same wooden floor can sound completely different. || Presenter: Have you ever used a surprising object for a sound? || Ben: Constantly. The actual object often makes a disappointing noise. For one adventure film, a character had to walk through deep snow. We weren’t in snow at all. [27]I squeezed bags filled with cornflour under my shoes because they produced the soft crunch the director wanted[/27]. It looked ridiculous in the studio, but it sounded convincing. || Presenter: What happens when a director does not like a sound? || Ben: You have to ask what is wrong, rather than defending your first idea. A door closing can feel funny, frightening or final. [28]Directors usually describe the emotion they want the audience to experience, and I then find a sound that creates that feeling[/28]. Realism is not always the only goal. || Presenter: What creates pressure in your job? || Ben: Time. We often record after the film has been edited, close to the deadline. [29]Repeating a sound at exactly the right moment while also changing its quality can be extremely demanding when time is limited[/29]. It requires concentration, but also the ability to laugh when a pile of objects falls over. || Presenter: What should beginners do? || Ben: Don’t rush out and buy equipment. [30]Begin by listening carefully to ordinary sounds around you and asking what information or emotion each one gives you[/30]. An excellent microphone is no use if you haven’t learned to hear creatively. || Presenter: Ben, thank you. || Ben: Thank you.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: How did Ben become interested in creating film sounds?

options.A: He helped make sound effects for a friend's animation.
options.B: He was taught about it while studying music.
options.C: He heard unusual noises during film editing.

questions.explanation: Ben created sounds for a friend's animation and enjoyed it more than his editing work.

questions.question: What does Ben say about his usual method of working?

options.A: He combines music with stored computer sounds.
options.B: He records objects while following the film action.
options.C: He collects outdoor noises before films are produced.

questions.explanation: He records real objects in time with the pictures instead of simply choosing stored sounds.

questions.question: Why can recording footsteps be difficult?

options.A: They have to communicate something about the character.
options.B: Actors frequently walk too quickly for the recording.
options.C: It is hard to find floors made from the right materials.

questions.explanation: Ben says footsteps must reveal a character's state, such as confidence or tiredness.

questions.question: What did Ben use cornflour to create?

options.A: The sound of an object being broken.
options.B: The sound of a character walking in snow.
options.C: The sound of food being prepared.

questions.explanation: Cornflour in bags created the soft crunch needed for footsteps in deep snow.

questions.question: How do directors usually explain the sound they require?

options.A: They show Ben an example from another film.
options.B: They tell him which real object to record.
options.C: They describe the feeling the sound should produce.

questions.explanation: Directors often express the emotion they want an audience to feel.

questions.question: What does Ben find demanding when deadlines are close?

options.A: Producing a sound with precise timing and the right character.
options.B: Working with directors who change the film's story.
options.C: Finding equipment available at short notice.

questions.explanation: He needs to repeat sounds at exactly the correct time while adjusting their quality.

questions.question: What first step does Ben suggest for beginners?

options.C: Pay close attention to everyday sounds.

questions.explanation: Ben believes beginners should first learn to listen creatively to ordinary noises.
